			want:     []string{"John", "Doe", "Smith"},

Purpose: Search for the first occurrence of any string in the provided list within the cleaned name parts.

Parameters:
- parts: A collection of strings to search for

Returns:
- The index of the first name part that matches (case-insensitive) any string in the 'parts' list
- -1 if no match is found

Behavior:
1. Iterate through each part of the cleaned name string
2. For each name part, compare it case-insensitively with each string in the 'parts' list
3. If a match is found, return the index of the matching name part
4. If no match is found after examining all parts, return -1

  "source_test_file_implementation": "package gonameparts

import (
	"testing"
)

func TestSearchPartsExtended(t *testing.T) {
	tests := []struct {
		name       string
		nameString *nameString
		parts      []string
		expected   int
	}{
		{
			name: "Match found at first position",
			nameString: &nameString{
				FullName: "John Doe",
			},
			parts:    []string{"John", "Jane"},
			expected: 0,
		},
		{
			name: "Match found at second position",
			nameString: &nameString{
				FullName: "John Doe",
			},
			parts:    []string{"Jane", "Doe"},
			expected: 1,
		},
		{
			name: "Match found with mixed case",
			nameString: &nameString{
				FullName: "John Doe",
			},
			parts:    []string{"doe", "jane"},
			expected: 1,
		},
		{
			name: "No match found",
			nameString: &nameString{
				FullName: "John Doe",
			},
			parts:    []string{"Jane", "Smith"},
			expected: -1,
		},
		{
			name: "Empty parts list",
			nameString: &nameString{
				FullName: "John Doe",
			},
			parts:    []string{},
			expected: -1,
		},
		{
			name: "Names with punctuation",
			nameString: &nameString{
				FullName: "John, Doe.",
			},
			parts:    []string{"John", "Doe"},
			expected: 0,
		},
	}

	for _, tt := range tests {
		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			result := tt.nameString.searchParts(tt.parts)
			if result != tt.expected {
				t.Errorf("searchParts() = %v, want %v", result, tt.expected)
			}
		})
	}
}",

  "target_test_file_implementation": "use crate::namestring::NameString;

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_search_parts() {
        // Test case: Match found at first position
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "John Doe".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: String::new(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        let parts = vec!["John".to_string(), "Jane".to_string()];
        assert_eq!(name_string.search_parts(&parts), 0);

        // Test case: Match found at second position
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "John Doe".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: String::new(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        let parts = vec!["Jane".to_string(), "Doe".to_string()];
        assert_eq!(name_string.search_parts(&parts), 1);

        // Test case: Match found with mixed case
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "John Doe".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: String::new(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        let parts = vec!["doe".to_string(), "jane".to_string()];
        assert_eq!(name_string.search_parts(&parts), 1);

        // Test case: No match found
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "John Doe".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: String::new(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        let parts = vec!["Jane".to_string(), "Smith".to_string()];
        assert_eq!(name_string.search_parts(&parts), -1);

        // Test case: Empty parts list
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "John Doe".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: String::new(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        let parts: Vec<String> = vec![];
        assert_eq!(name_string.search_parts(&parts), -1);

        // Test case: Names with punctuation
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "John, Doe.".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: String::new(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        let parts = vec!["John".to_string(), "Doe".to_string()];
        assert_eq!(name_string.search_parts(&parts), 0);
    }
}",

		},

			partToFind:     "suffix",
			expectedResult: 2,
		},
		{

			partToFind:     "lnprefix",
			expectedResult: 1,
		},

			input:          "John Doe AKA Johnny",
			partToFind:     "nonname",
			expectedResult: 2,
		},

The Go version iterates through each index of n.SplitName and checks if it exists in the slotted array using a nested loop. For each index i, it sets a 'found' flag to false, then checks each element j in slotted to see if i equals j. If found, it sets the flag to true and breaks. If not found after checking all slotted elements, it appends i to notSlotted. The Rust version achieves the same result more efficiently by using the contains() method on slotted to check if an index exists. It iterates through each element of self.split_name with its index i, and if i is not in slotted, it adds it to not_slotted. While the implementation details differ (nested loop vs. contains()), both algorithms have the same functionality: they iterate through all indices of the name string and collect those not present in the slotted array. The only formal difference is that the Rust version wraps the result in an Ok() for error handling, but this doesn't affect the core algorithm's behavior."
}

  "correct_target_method_implementation": "impl NameString {
    pub(crate) fn fix_misplaced_apostrophe(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        let mut ends_with_apostrophe = Vec::new();
        for (index, x) in self.split().iter().enumerate() {
            if x.ends_with('\'') {
                ends_with_apostrophe.push(index);
            }
        }
        if !ends_with_apostrophe.is_empty() {
            // In the Go implementation, only the first apostrophe is processed
            let y = ends_with_apostrophe[0];
            if self.split_name[y] == self.split_name[self.split_name.len() - 1] {
                let mut tmp_name = self.split_name[..y].to_vec();
                tmp_name.push(self.split_name[y].trim_matches('\'').to_string());
                self.full_name = tmp_name.join(" ");
            } else {
                let misplaced_start = y;
                let mut fixed_name = vec![self.split_name[misplaced_start].clone()];
                fixed_name.push(self.split_name[misplaced_start + 1].clone());
                let fixed_placement = fixed_name.join("");
                let mut tmp_name = self.split_name[..misplaced_start].to_vec();
                tmp_name.push(fixed_placement);
                tmp_name.extend_from_slice(&self.split_name[misplaced_start + 2..]);
                self.full_name = tmp_name.join(" ");
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}"
}

  "source_test_file_implementation": "package gonameparts

import (
	"testing"
)

func TestHasAliases(t *testing.T) {
	testCases := []struct {
		name           string
		fullName       string
		expectedHas    bool
		expectedSep    string
	}{
		{
			name:        "No aliases",
			fullName:    "John Smith",
			expectedHas: false,
			expectedSep: "",
		},
		{
			name:        "With AKA in middle",
			fullName:    "John AKA Johnny Smith",
			expectedHas: true,
			expectedSep: "AKA",
		},
		{
			name:        "With A.K.A in middle",
			fullName:    "John A.K.A Johnny Smith",
			expectedHas: true,
			expectedSep: "A.K.A",
		},
		{
			name:        "With FKA in middle",
			fullName:    "Jane FKA Smith Jones",
			expectedHas: true,
			expectedSep: "FKA",
		},
		{
			name:        "With alias at end - should not be detected",
			fullName:    "John Smith AKA",
			expectedHas: false,
			expectedSep: "",
		},
		{
			name:        "With lowercase alias - should be case insensitive",
			fullName:    "John aka Johnny Smith",
			expectedHas: true,
			expectedSep: "AKA",
		},
		{
			name:        "With mixed case alias",
			fullName:    "John aKa Johnny Smith",
			expectedHas: true, 
			expectedSep: "AKA",
		},
		{
			name:        "With N/K/A alias",
			fullName:    "John N/K/A Johnny Smith",
			expectedHas: true,
			expectedSep: "N/K/A",
		},
	}

	for _, tc := range testCases {
		t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			n := &nameString{
				FullName: tc.fullName,
			}
			
			hasAlias, sep := n.hasAliases()
			
			if hasAlias != tc.expectedHas {
				t.Errorf("Expected hasAlias to be %v, got %v", tc.expectedHas, hasAlias)
			}
			
			if sep != tc.expectedSep {
				t.Errorf("Expected separator to be %q, got %q", tc.expectedSep, sep)
			}
		})
	}
}",

  "target_test_file_implementation": "#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use crate::nameparts::NON_NAME;
    use crate::namestring::NameString;

    #[test]
    fn test_has_aliases() {
        let test_cases = vec![
            (
                "No aliases", 
                "John Smith", 
                false, 
                String::new()
            ),
            (
                "With AKA in middle", 
                "John AKA Johnny Smith", 
                true, 
                "AKA".to_string()
            ),
            (
                "With A.K.A in middle", 
                "John A.K.A Johnny Smith", 
                true, 
                "A.K.A".to_string()
            ),
            (
                "With FKA in middle", 
                "Jane FKA Smith Jones", 
                true, 
                "FKA".to_string()
            ),
            (
                "With alias at end - should not be detected", 
                "John Smith AKA", 
                false, 
                String::new()
            ),
            (
                "With lowercase alias - should be case insensitive", 
                "John aka Johnny Smith", 
                true, 
                "AKA".to_string()
            ),
            (
                "With mixed case alias", 
                "John aKa Johnny Smith", 
                true, 
                "AKA".to_string()
            ),
            (
                "With N/K/A alias", 
                "John N/K/A Johnny Smith", 
                true, 
                "N/K/A".to_string()
            ),
        ];

        for (name, full_name, expected_has, expected_sep) in test_cases {
            let n = NameString {
                full_name: full_name.to_string(),
                ..Default::default()
            };
            
            let (has_alias, sep) = n.has_aliases();
            
            assert_eq!(
                has_alias, 
                expected_has, 
                "{}: Expected has_alias to be {}, got {}", 
                name, expected_has, has_alias
            );
            
            assert_eq!(
                sep, 
                expected_sep, 
                "{}: Expected separator to be {}, got {}", 
                name, expected_sep, sep
            );
        }
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_non_name_constants() {
        // This test verifies that the NON_NAME constants used by has_aliases
        // include all expected alias separators
        let expected_separators = [
            "A.K.A", "AKA", "A/K/A", "F.K.A", "FKA", "F/K/A", "N/K/A"
        ];

        for &sep in &expected_separators {
            assert!(
                NON_NAME.contains(&sep),
                "Expected NON_NAME to contain '{}', but it doesn't", 
                sep
            );
        }
    }
}",

            split_name: vec!["Polera,".to_string(), "James".to_string()],
            nickname: "".to_string(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };

        // Test with a name without any commas
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "James Polera".to_string(),
            split_name: vec!["James".to_string(), "Polera".to_string()],
            nickname: "".to_string(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        
        let result = name_string.has_comma();
        assert!(!result, "Expected false for name without comma");
    }

    #[test]

        // Test with a comma in the middle of a name part
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "James Po,lera".to_string(),
            split_name: vec!["James".to_string(), "Po,lera".to_string()],
            nickname: "".to_string(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        
        let result = name_string.has_comma();
        assert!(result, "Expected true for name with comma in the middle");
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_has_comma_empty() {
        // Test with an empty name
        let mut name_string = NameString {
            full_name: "".to_string(),
            split_name: vec![],
            nickname: "".to_string(),
            aliases: vec![],
        };
        
        let result = name_string.has_comma();
        assert!(!result, "Expected false for empty name");
    }
}",

func TestNormalizeFunction(t *testing.T) {
	tests := []struct {
		name     string
		fullName string
		expected []string
	}{
		{
			name:     "Basic name normalization",
			fullName: "John Doe",
			expected: []string{"John", "Doe"},
		},
		{
			name:     "With supplemental info",
			fullName: "John Doe MD",
			expected: []string{"John", "Doe"},
		},
		{
			name:     "With nickname",
			fullName: "John 'Johnny' Doe",
			expected: []string{"John", "Doe"},
		},
		{
			name:     "With misplaced apostrophe",
			fullName: "O' Brien",
			expected: []string{"OBrien"},
		},
		{
			name:     "With comma",
			fullName: "Doe, John",
			expected: []string{"John", "Doe"},
		},
		{
			name:     "With alias",
			fullName: "John Doe AKA Johnny",
			expected: []string{"John", "Doe"},
		},
		{
			name:     "Complex case",
			fullName: "Doe, John 'Johnny' MD",
			expected: []string{"John", "Doe"},
		},
	}

	for _, tt := range tests {
		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {

The key issue is in error handling for the find(\"supplemental\") method call. In the Go implementation, the function directly checks if the supplemental index is greater than -1, while the Rust implementation conditionally unwraps the Result using `if let Ok(supplemental_index)` pattern matching. This causes different behavior when find() returns an error - the Rust version would skip the supplemental info processing entirely whereas the Go version would always check the index. The test execution outcomes clearly demonstrated these differences with specific test failures in the Rust implementation."
}

1. Error handling: The Go version returns a NameParts struct directly without error handling, while the Rust version returns Result<NameParts, anyhow::Error>, propagating errors that might occur during parsing.

2. Method calls: The Rust version explicitly handles potential errors from method calls like n.normalize(), n.find(), n.find_not_slotted(), and p.build_full_name() with '?' operator, which could lead to early returns with errors. The Go version assumes these methods never fail.

3. Return type: The Go function always returns a NameParts struct, while the Rust function returns a Result wrapping the NameParts. This means the Rust function can terminate early with an error while the Go function will always return a potentially incomplete NameParts.

}

While both functions aim to parse a name string into its components, there are several key differences in their I/O behavior:

1. Error handling: The Go version returns a NameParts struct directly without error handling, while the Rust version returns Result<NameParts, anyhow::Error>, propagating errors that might occur during parsing.

- Output: A structured NameParts object containing the parsed components

Behavior:

2. If the name appears to be a corporate/business name, return the minimal NameParts with just the provided name
3. Identify and extract special parts in this order: generation, suffix, last name prefix, supplemental
4. Identify salutation if it's at the beginning of the name
5. Identify non-name parts if they're not at the end of the name

7. Handle prefixed last names specially by joining them with other relevant parts
8. Identify middle and last name components from remaining unslotted parts:
   - If multiple unslotted parts and no last name prefix: the last one is the last name, others are middle name
   - If multiple unslotted parts with last name prefix: all are middle name
   - If only one unslotted part: assign to last name unless there's a last name prefix

Function Parse(name: String) -> NameParts:

- Input: A string representing a person's name
- Output: A structured NameParts object containing the parsed components

Behavior:
1. Normalize the input name string (trim, handle special characters)
2. If the name appears to be a corporate/business name, return the minimal NameParts with just the provided name
3. Identify and extract special parts in this order: generation, suffix, last name prefix, supplemental
4. Identify salutation if it's at the beginning of the name
5. Identify non-name parts if they're not at the end of the name
6. Identify first name as the part after salutation
7. Handle prefixed last names specially by joining them with other relevant parts
8. Identify middle and last name components from remaining unslotted parts:
   - If multiple unslotted parts and no last name prefix: the last one is the last name, others are middle name
   - If multiple unslotted parts with last name prefix: all are middle name
   - If only one unslotted part: assign to last name unless there's a last name prefix
9. Process any aliases recursively
10. Build a full name representation from the parsed components
11. Return the complete NameParts structure

        assert_eq!(result.first_name, "James");

  "correct_target_method_implementation": "pub fn parse(name: &str) -> Result<NameParts, anyhow::Error> {
    let mut n = NameString {
        full_name: name.to_string(),
        ..Default::default()
    };
    n.normalize()?;

    let mut p = NameParts {
        provided_name: name.to_string(),
        nickname: n.nickname.clone(),
        ..Default::default()
    };

    // If we're dealing with a business name, just return it back
    if n.looks_corporate() {
        return Ok(p);
    }

    let parts = vec!["generation", "suffix", "lnprefix", "supplemental"];
    let mut part_map: HashMap<&str, i32> = HashMap::new();
    let mut slotted: Vec<usize> = Vec::new();

    // Slot and index parts
    for part in &parts {
        let part_index = n.find(part)?;
        part_map.insert(part, part_index);
        if part_index > -1 {
            p.slot(part, &n.split_name[part_index as usize]);
            slotted.push(part_index as usize);
        }
    }

    // Find salutation, but make sure it's first; otherwise it may be a false positive
    if let Ok(sal_index) = n.find("salutation") {
        if sal_index == 0 {
            part_map.insert("salutation", sal_index);
            p.slot("salutation", &n.split_name[sal_index as usize]);
            slotted.push(sal_index as usize);
        } else {
            part_map.insert("salutation", -1);
        }
    } else {
        part_map.insert("salutation", -1);
    }

    // Find nonname, but make sure it's not last; otherwise it may be a false positive
    if let Ok(nn_index) = n.find("nonname") {
        if nn_index > -1 && nn_index < (n.split_name.len() - 1) as i32 {
            part_map.insert("nonname", nn_index);
            p.slot("nonname", &n.split_name[nn_index as usize]);
            slotted.push(nn_index as usize);
        } else {
            part_map.insert("nonname", -1);
        }
    } else {
        part_map.insert("nonname", -1);
    }

    // Slot FirstName
    let first_pos = *part_map.get("salutation").unwrap_or(&-1) + 1;
    if first_pos == n.split_name.len() as i32 {
        p.build_full_name()?;
        return Ok(p);
    }
    part_map.insert("first", first_pos);
    p.slot("first", &n.split_name[first_pos as usize]);
    slotted.push(first_pos as usize);

    // Slot prefixed LastName
    if let Some(&ln_prefix) = part_map.get("lnprefix") {
        if ln_prefix > -1 {
            let mut ln_end = n.split_name.len();
            if let Some(&generation) = part_map.get("generation") {
                if generation > -1 {
                    ln_end = generation as usize;
                }
            }
            if let Some(&suffix) = part_map.get("suffix") {
                if suffix > -1 && (suffix as usize) < ln_end {
                    ln_end = suffix as usize;
                }
            }
            // Need to validate the slice parameters make sense
            // Example Name: "I am the Popsicle"
            // This example causes a hit on the generation at position 0,
            // which in turn causes lnEnd to be set to 0, but the lnprefix
            // is greater than 0, causing a slice out of bounds panic
            if ln_end > ln_prefix as usize {
                let last_name: Vec<&str> = n
                    .split_name[ln_prefix as usize..ln_end]
                    .iter()
                    .map(|s| s.as_str())
                    .collect();
                p.slot("last", &last_name.join(" "));
            }

            // Keep track of what we've slotted
            for i in ln_prefix as usize..=ln_end {
                slotted.push(i);
            }
        }
    }

    // Slot the rest
    let not_slotted = n.find_not_slotted(&slotted)?;

    if not_slotted.len() > 1 {
        let ln_prefix = *part_map.get("lnprefix").unwrap_or(&-1);
        let mut multi_middle = Vec::new();
        if ln_prefix > -1 {
            for p in &not_slotted {
                multi_middle.push(n.split_name[*p].clone());
            }
            p.slot("middle", &multi_middle.join(" "));
        } else {
            let mut not_slotted_sorted = not_slotted.clone();
            not_slotted_sorted.sort_unstable();
            let max_not_slotted_index = *not_slotted_sorted.last().unwrap();
            p.slot("last", &n.split_name[max_not_slotted_index]);

            for p in &not_slotted {
                if *p != max_not_slotted_index {
                    multi_middle.push(n.split_name[*p].clone());
                }
            }
            p.slot("middle", &multi_middle.join(" "));
        }
    }

    if not_slotted.len() == 1 {
        if *part_map.get("lnprefix").unwrap_or(&-1) > -1 {
            p.slot("middle", &n.split_name[not_slotted[0]]);
        } else {
            p.slot("last", &n.split_name[not_slotted[0]]);
        }
    }

    for alias in &n.aliases {
        // Handle potential errors in recursive alias parsing
        // In the Go implementation, errors are not propagated
        if let Ok(alias_parts) = parse(alias) {
            p.aliases.push(alias_parts);
        }
    }
    p.build_full_name()?;
    Ok(p)
}"
}

  "correct_target_method_implementation": "pub fn parse(name: &str) -> Result<NameParts, anyhow::Error> {
    let mut n = NameString {
        full_name: name.to_string(),
        ..Default::default()
    };
    n.normalize()?;

    let mut p = NameParts {
        provided_name: name.to_string(),
        nickname: n.nickname.clone(),
        ..Default::default()
    };

    // If we're dealing with a business name, just return it back
    if n.looks_corporate() {
        return Ok(p);
    }

    let parts = vec!["generation", "suffix", "lnprefix", "supplemental"];
    let mut part_map: HashMap<&str, i32> = HashMap::new();
    let mut slotted: Vec<usize> = Vec::new();

    // Slot and index parts
    for part in &parts {
        let part_index = n.find(part)?;
        part_map.insert(part, part_index);
        if part_index > -1 {
            p.slot(part, &n.split_name[part_index as usize]);
            slotted.push(part_index as usize);
        }
    }

    // Find salutation, but make sure it's first; otherwise it may be a false positive
    if let Ok(sal_index) = n.find("salutation") {
        if sal_index == 0 {
            part_map.insert("salutation", sal_index);
            p.slot("salutation", &n.split_name[sal_index as usize]);
            slotted.push(sal_index as usize);
        } else {
            part_map.insert("salutation", -1);
        }
    } else {
        part_map.insert("salutation", -1);
    }

    // Find nonname, but make sure it's not last; otherwise it may be a false positive
    if let Ok(nn_index) = n.find("nonname") {
        if nn_index > -1 && nn_index < (n.split_name.len() - 1) as i32 {
            part_map.insert("nonname", nn_index);
            p.slot("nonname", &n.split_name[nn_index as usize]);
            slotted.push(nn_index as usize);
        } else {
            part_map.insert("nonname", -1);
        }
    } else {
        part_map.insert("nonname", -1);
    }

    // Slot FirstName
    let first_pos = *part_map.get("salutation").unwrap_or(&-1) + 1;
    if first_pos == n.split_name.len() as i32 {
        p.build_full_name()?;
        return Ok(p);
    }
    part_map.insert("first", first_pos);
    p.slot("first", &n.split_name[first_pos as usize]);
    slotted.push(first_pos as usize);

    // Slot prefixed LastName
    if let Some(&ln_prefix) = part_map.get("lnprefix") {
        if ln_prefix > -1 {
            let mut ln_end = n.split_name.len();
            if let Some(&generation) = part_map.get("generation") {
                if generation > -1 {
                    ln_end = generation as usize;
                }
            }
            if let Some(&suffix) = part_map.get("suffix") {
                if suffix > -1 && (suffix as usize) < ln_end {
                    ln_end = suffix as usize;
                }
            }
            // Need to validate the slice parameters make sense
            // Example Name: "I am the Popsicle"
            // This example causes a hit on the generation at position 0,
            // which in turn causes lnEnd to be set to 0, but the lnprefix
            // is greater than 0, causing a slice out of bounds panic
            if ln_end > ln_prefix as usize {
                let last_name: Vec<&str> = n
                    .split_name[ln_prefix as usize..ln_end]
                    .iter()
                    .map(|s| s.as_str())
                    .collect();
                p.slot("last", &last_name.join(" "));
            }

            // Keep track of what we've slotted
            for i in ln_prefix as usize..=ln_end {
                slotted.push(i);
            }
        }
    }

    // Slot the rest
    let not_slotted = n.find_not_slotted(&slotted)?;

    if not_slotted.len() > 1 {
        let ln_prefix = *part_map.get("lnprefix").unwrap_or(&-1);
        let mut multi_middle = Vec::new();
        if ln_prefix > -1 {
            for p in &not_slotted {
                multi_middle.push(n.split_name[*p].clone());
            }
            p.slot("middle", &multi_middle.join(" "));
        } else {
            let mut not_slotted_sorted = not_slotted.clone();
            not_slotted_sorted.sort_unstable();
            let max_not_slotted_index = *not_slotted_sorted.last().unwrap();
            p.slot("last", &n.split_name[max_not_slotted_index]);

            for p in &not_slotted {
                if *p != max_not_slotted_index {
                    multi_middle.push(n.split_name[*p].clone());
                }
            }
            p.slot("middle", &multi_middle.join(" "));
        }
    }

    if not_slotted.len() == 1 {
        if *part_map.get("lnprefix").unwrap_or(&-1) > -1 {
            p.slot("middle", &n.split_name[not_slotted[0]]);
        } else {
            p.slot("last", &n.split_name[not_slotted[0]]);
        }
    }

    for alias in &n.aliases {
        // Handle potential errors in recursive alias parsing
        // In the Go implementation, errors are not propagated
        if let Ok(alias_parts) = parse(alias) {
            p.aliases.push(alias_parts);
        }
    }
    p.build_full_name()?;
    Ok(p)
}"

parsed_final_response.explanation: After reviewing all analyses, I conclude that the Go and Rust implementations of the name parsing function are not functionally equivalent. The primary issue is in the error handling approach, particularly with recursive alias parsing. The Go implementation always processes aliases without error propagation, while the Rust implementation uses the '?' operator which can cause early termination when an alias fails to parse. The test results confirm this difference, showing failures in the Rust implementation's handling of aliases (specifically differences in the .ProvidedName field of aliases and how empty alias arrays are handled). Additionally, the test repair analysis identified this exact issue and provided a fix by modifying the Rust code to continue processing even when an alias can't be parsed, similar to the Go version's approach. The semantic analyses also flagged this difference in error handling and I/O behavior. While the control flow, data flow, and library equivalence analyses found similarities in the overall algorithm, the critical functional difference in alias error handling means the implementations are not equivalent in their actual behavior when processing names with complex aliases.
